item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well I now have a strong contender for my favourite SCP. +1 for your nautical nightmare!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite SCPs on the site to be perfectly honest. The idea is so cool, and the extra stuff (Incident reports, Addendums) make it feel real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 the dialogue bit keeps me afraid of the dark weeks after reading it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We're whalers on the moon
We carry a harpoon
But their ain't no whale
So we tell tall tales and sing a whaling tune

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since it is contained in a harbor and the page makes no mention at all of the foundation giving it live whales to "feed" upon, one might think that as the months go by this skip may sink when enough of the organic matter it has used for structure rots away.

So now the Ethics Committee has to figure out if the skip should be fed live whales or not, and that's what really gets under my skin as icing on the horror cake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rereading this, I'm struck by how much characterization is packed into that pair of sentences about its "trophies".

That's the difference between a thing that's just hunting to ensure its own survival, and something capable of sadism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this was posted years ago, but I just saw this and I just finished playing Dishonored… so… represent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Ahab's Antagonists" may be my new favorite MTF name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the last note is a bit cliche by modern standards, and once again, the concept is another biological machine. But I like this one; the last note wasn't there just because, the boat was going crazy without meat. It's not entirely unique, but this has decent enough execution for me to upvote:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was disturbing to say the least.
Take your stupid upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't like this at all. If it was a ship that captures people and makes itself out of them it'd be cliché as all hell and generally hated… changing it to whales does nothing for me.

Plus the picture made me feel nauseous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All these motherfing ships want to be alive! Creepy stuff! Nice work here!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Containment has come to include basic maintenance, because letting something die is just as much against the creed as actively destroying it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I presume it's rotting. They're just trying to contain it, not keep it in tip-top shape. I'd assume that in-lore this boat would be a lower-priority (And thus lower budget for containment) than most euclids, such as 173.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup, i'd say it was my second favourite, now, after 1440.

item.title: Re: To Author
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I think 511 is the only really good first SCP i've read. No need to beat yourself up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'Go home, SCP-1245, you're drunk'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whale, these puns get worse every year. But oh whale, what the hell….when in Wales

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mental imagery of the concept at work and insides of the ship proper were suitably horrifying (seriously, a corpse-fed extinction ship cruising the seas? damn), and most of the critique on my part has already been stated as is.

Upvote +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, wow… thank you. Thank you very much.

item.title: Re: Hold on a mo.
item.content:encoded: This post caused tears to manifest in the ocular regions of Author Azzleflux

item.title: Re: Hold on a mo.
item.content:encoded: 110-Montauk is the recorded sound of SCP-231's mother, screaming for her lost child.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now its my favorite SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What am I missing here? It's a ship that hunt whales and is made of whale parts. Yeah well, that doesn't really push any buttons for me.
Also "Rotting or decaying parts are replaced with new components as new whale specimens are acquired and processed."

Okay so what happens if it doesn't come into contact with any fresh whale specimens? In other words, how does it even survive its current containment?

item.title: Re: Biology nitpick
item.content:encoded: Fixed. Thanks for the advice.

item.title: Biology nitpick
item.content:encoded:
Unusual specimens gathered by SCP-1245 include dolphins (Delphinidae spp.), orca whales, (Orcinus orca) beaked whales (Ziphiidae spp.), narwhals, and belugas (Monodontidae spp.).

As it now stands, the use of italics with scientific names is mostly incorrect. There are also some misplaced commas. Here's my suggested fix:

Unusual specimens gathered by SCP-1245 include dolphins (family Delphinidae), orca whales (Orcinus orca), beaked whales (family Ziphiidae), and narwhals and belugas (family Monodontidae).

1) Family names are never italicized.
2) The abbreviation "spp." is generally not italicized, though this convention varies by journal seeing as it is Latin.
3) While the abbreviation "spp." can be used to refer to species within a family, it would be fairly awkward to do so here without getting too wordy. One doesn't often see "Delphindae spp." for example.

Seeing as these corrections are a bit more than just simple grammar, I thought I'd post a comment instead of making the change myself. If you'd like advice on other ways to indicate scientific names, feel free to PM me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I won't lie, I would've upvoted this -just- because it killed some Sea Shepherds. Then it turns out to be made of whale. Even better. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DID YOU JUST MENTION 682 IN CONTEXT WITH ANOTHER SCP?! HERETIC!

Seriously though, that'd be pretty funny. I'm curious too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed. Thanks for the heads-up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One minor nitpick:

Foundation ships usually carry the designation SCPS, not USS.

Other than that, this is a really creepy concept, and I think it's done well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it's rotting, and uses whales to fix itself, shouldn't there be something about occasionally "feeding" it?

That picture is horrible and bears no relationship to the anomaly. The whale is a victim, such photos would certainly be in the full report, but attached as the equivalent of the boats mug shot? I don't think so. Just comes across as cheap shock value.

I love that the horrific, insatiable meat-machine monster is not targeting humans, for once. It's a big ecosystem, but you wouldn't know that by the average scps dietary habits.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The transmitted message was just shy of goofy to me, but somehow it made the article. The incident report just made it better.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pickled narwhals are better at Morse code than I would be. :U

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OH.

I think I missed the size of this boat. Nevermind!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It'd probably be more of a hassle to try and drag a 120-meter long ship onto the land and move it by truck to a secure location (and possibly get harpooned to death in the process) then to just shut it in an enclosed harbor and chain it down. That's my rationale, anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow this is creepy as fuck. Been a while since I saw a good "creepy as fuck" article.

My only question is, why don't they just pull it out of the water and store it somewhere on land?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *wails*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't undersand why people are getting irritated. Perhaps they're trolling people on porpoise?

item.title: Re: Hold on a mo.
item.content:encoded: I have no qualms about this theory. It just makes this thing even more batshit insane, which I welcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was extremely 'meh' about this until I read the incident report.
*thing basically eats vigilante boat*
"What is this, actual metal used to make ships? Fuck that."
*Tosses it overboard*
EDIT: If that was unclear, I enjoyed it by the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might be too finicky, but I'm assuming that the SCP primarily operated in the Southern Pacific Ocean, where most commercial whaling takes place.
This area makes the hunting of Belugas and Narwhals questionable.
Both are commonly found in the Arctic Ocean, and Belugas stray into the Pacific and Atlantic, but Narwhals almost exclusively live on the Atlantic side of things. If the ship will circumnavigate the globe to hunt, you might want to make a note of its former range.

Also, from what I've seen, Foundation ships have both an internal name and a cover name.

+1 anyways; I'm just picking nits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just for the lulz, I'd be tempted to slather 682 in whale oil and then toss the lizard into the ship.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beach, beach, beach, that's all you ever do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're just blowing hot air.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Puns? Really? Sea, you're the reason we can't have nice things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, I don't think the author will be able to write a second one as good as this; this was clearly a fluke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes it was a gripping tail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You mean you had a whale of a time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP was so sad I wailed at the end.

item.title: Re: Hold on a mo.
item.content:encoded: often

item.title: Re: Hold on a mo.
item.content:encoded:
I DON'T EVEN KNOW WHAT MY REACTIONS ARE DOING.

e: Wait, wait, yes I do. Headcanoned forever.

ee: Mark my words, there will be a tale about this.

item.title: Hold on a mo.
item.content:encoded: SCP-231-7 is often thought to be a whale. This is a ship that captures whales and uses them in repairing itself. What if, at one point, this thing captured all instances of SCP-231? What if this thing was created by the Children of the Scarlet King, and after outliving it's usefulness (capturing all 7 instances of SCP-231) roamed the seas for years, and found a way to repair itself through sacrificial magic involving whales?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yo dawg, I heard you like whales so we put whales in your whaling boat so you can whale while you whale… in Wales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I…..I love this thing.

A mighty +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry! I'm just kind of compelled to put SCPs in the first available [ACCESS DENIED] slot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ach, now I'll have to change all the numbers in my Inca Robo-priest rewrite.

Cuuuurrrseee yooooou! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The bit about "grammar and spelling remain identical" was only put in there because I couldn't think of a better way to lead into the rant. Other than that, no. Presumably it's incoherent because it's angry, or because its' using a pickled narwhal brain to try and speak English through Morse code.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Put him in charge of an Exxon tanker,
Put him in charge of an Exxon tanker,
Put him in charge of an Exxon tanker,
Put him in charge of an Exxon tanker, early in the morning…

Way-hey, and up she rises
Way-hey, and up she rises
Way-hey, and up she rises, early in the morning….

item.title: Re: To Author
item.content:encoded: My first SCP was God awful as well. It's a rare person who can make their first SCP a good one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was the second addendum inspired by SCP-962?
